metadata.title: Shakespeare Weather Concordance (Merged)
metadata.description: Unified canonical concordance merging curated passages and literal/metaphorical entries from scene-by-scene analysis

concordance.text: Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks! rage! blow! You cataracts and hurricanoes, spout till you have drench'd our steeples, drown'd the cocks!
concordance.speaker: Lear
concordance.context: Lear, cast out by his daughters, commands the storm to rage on the heath. He externalizes his inner fury onto the natural world.
concordance.annotation: The storm is both literal and metaphorical—Lear's madness mirrors the tempest. His imperative commands to the winds suggest he believes himself master of nature, even as it destroys him.

concordance.text: Here's a night pities neither wise men nor fools. Crumb no more, and this poor thing dismiss; come, let's in all.
concordance.speaker: Kent
concordance.context: Kent urges Lear to seek shelter from the terrible night. The physical storm mirrors Lear's psychological tempest.
concordance.annotation: Kent's practical concern contrasts with Lear's defiant rage—the same storm means danger to Kent, tragedy to Lear, and revelation to the audience.

concordance.text: There was never yet philosopher that could endure the toothache patiently; but a man may rail against the gods and make the night hideous.
concordance.speaker: Fool
concordance.context: The Fool comments on human suffering during the tempest. Even the night itself seems alive with Lear's suffering.
concordance.annotation: The Fool's wisdom cuts through the storm—the night is not merely dark, but made 'hideous' by human consciousness and pain.

concordance.text: A tempestuous noise of thunder and lightning heard. Enter a SHIPMASTER and a BOATSWAIN.
concordance.speaker: Stage direction
concordance.context: The play opens with a violent storm at sea. This supernatural tempest, conjured by Prospero, sets the entire plot in motion.
concordance.annotation: The tempest is both meteorological and magical—the boundary between nature and art collapses at the very threshold of the play.

concordance.text: I have bedimm'd the noontide sun, call'd forth the mutinous winds, and 'twixt the green sea and the azured vault set roaring war.
concordance.speaker: Prospero
concordance.context: Prospero reveals to Miranda that he conjured the storm through magic. He claims dominion over the very elements.
concordance.annotation: Prospero's syntax mirrors incantation—the rhythm and vocabulary of power. His storm is simultaneously creation and curse.

concordance.text: All torment, trouble, wonder and amazement Inhabits here: some heavenly power guide us Out of this fearful country!
concordance.speaker: Alonso
concordance.context: Alonso, shipwrecked, finds himself on the island. The storm has passed but left him disoriented in an alien landscape.
concordance.annotation: The 'fearful country' personifies the island as threatening—weather is psychological as well as physical.

concordance.text: The sun will set, and then the dew will dry all sorrow from your sight and hearing.
concordance.speaker: Prospero
concordance.context: As the play nears its conclusion, Prospero invokes the dew of evening. The weather shifts toward calm and reconciliation.
concordance.annotation: Dew as natural restorative—the play's movement is from storm to clarity, from chaos to order marked by changing light.

concordance.text: Thunder. Enter the three WITCHES.
concordance.speaker: Stage direction
concordance.context: The Witches appear in a thunderstorm. Their supernatural malevolence is announced through atmospheric disturbance.
concordance.annotation: Thunder as the voice of fate—the play's darkness is heralded by noise and storm, not light.

concordance.text: So foul and fair a day I have not seen.
concordance.speaker: Macbeth
concordance.context: Macbeth arrives at the heath where the Witches await. His first words echo their paradoxical language about fair and foul.
concordance.annotation: The oxymoronic weather mirrors the play's moral ambiguity—fair and foul are indistinguishable, just as goodness and evil blur.

concordance.text: It was a rough night.
concordance.speaker: Macduff
concordance.context: After Duncan's murder, the night was turbulent. Nature itself recoils from the regicide.
concordance.annotation: The understatement is Shakespearean—'rough' carries weight beyond meteorology. The natural world protests Macbeth's crime.

concordance.text: It will have blood, they say: blood will have blood.
concordance.speaker: Macbeth
concordance.context: Macbeth's paranoia deepens. The night becomes a space of violence and consequence, where blood calls for blood.
concordance.annotation: Darkness is not merely absence of light—it's the space where violent necessity unfolds, where fate operates beyond human vision.

concordance.text: Thunder. Enter HECATE and three other WITCHES.
concordance.speaker: Stage direction
concordance.context: The Witches gather in their cavern as thunder marks their assembly. The supernatural and meteorological blur.
concordance.annotation: Thunder signals not god but witchcraft—the hierarchy of power is inverted in Macbeth's world.

concordance.text: A light in a window. Enter a DOCTOR of Physic and a WAITING-GENTLEWOMAN.
concordance.speaker: Stage direction
concordance.context: The night is clear but Lady Macbeth walks in her madness. Light cannot dispel the darkness of conscience.
concordance.annotation: Even a clear night offers no comfort—the play's moral darkness persists regardless of meteorological conditions.

concordance.text: I know a bank where the wild thyme blows, Where oxlips and the nodding violet grows, Quite over-canopied with luscious woodbine.
concordance.speaker: Puck
concordance.context: Puck describes a secret place in the forest where Oberon and Titania meet. The night is luminous with magic.
concordance.annotation: The weather is latent in the description—verdant and moistly perfumed, the forest itself generates its own humid, fertile atmosphere.

concordance.text: Lord, what fools these mortals be! The moon's an arrant thief, and her pale fire she snatches from the sun.
concordance.speaker: Puck
concordance.context: Puck surveys the chaos of the lovers below under the moon. His commentary is cosmic and mischievous.
concordance.annotation: Moonlight is theft, reflection, and artifice—perfectly suited to Puck's world where identity and love are constantly misaligned.

concordance.text: Good morrow, friends. Saint Valentine is past: begin these wood-birds but to couple now?
concordance.speaker: Theseus
concordance.context: Dawn breaks over the forest. The enchanted night gives way to clarity and the lovers awake from their confusion.
concordance.annotation: The progression from night to dawn mirrors moral clarification—what was hidden becomes visible, what was chaotic becomes orderly.

concordance.text: If music be the food of love, play on; give me excess of it, that, surfeiting, the appetite may sicken, and so die.
concordance.speaker: Orsino
concordance.context: Orsino opens the play in a state of romantic melancholy, indulging in his own sorrow. The weather of his mind is gray.
concordance.annotation: The play's opening atmosphere is interiority—the weather is psychological, not meteorological, yet colors the entire mood.

concordance.text: What country, friends, is this? This is Illyria, lady.
concordance.speaker: Viola, Captain
concordance.context: Viola emerges from a shipwreck on the coast of Illyria. The storm has scattered her from her brother and left her stranded.
concordance.annotation: The storm, like Prospero's tempest, is a device of plot—maritime weather creates exile and new identity.

concordance.text: That strain again! it had a dying fall: O, it came o'er my ear like the sweet sound, that breathes upon a bank of violets.
concordance.speaker: Orsino
concordance.context: Orsino is entranced by music. The metaphor describes soft, perfumed air—the sensory weather of romance.
concordance.annotation: Weather as synesthesia—sound becomes breeze, becomes scent. The play's primary atmosphere is sensory and interior.

concordance.text: I am gone for ever!
concordance.speaker: Antigonus (exit pursued by bear)
concordance.context: Antigonus abandons baby Perdita on the wild coast of Bohemia, pursued by a bear. The seacoast is violent and unforgiving.
concordance.annotation: The bear and the storm are both metaphors for nature's indifference—the weather of tragedy is untamed wilderness.

concordance.text: The web of our life is of a mingled yarn, good and ill together.
concordance.speaker: Autolycus
concordance.context: Autolycus sings of life's mixture. The Bohemian pastoral is pleasant but never entirely clear.
concordance.annotation: The play's second half is weather of reconciliation—not clear, but not stormy. Mixed, like character itself.

concordance.text: Lawn as white as driven snow; Cyprus black as e'er was crow; Gloves as sweet as damask roses; Masks for faces and for noses.
concordance.speaker: Autolycus
concordance.context: Autolycus hawks his wares at the Bohemian festival. His song catalogues colors and textures against the winter backdrop.
concordance.annotation: Snow is commodity and metaphor—whiteness as purity and as blank slate, the canvas on which the play's restoration unfolds.

concordance.text: The winter's tale it is reported, for never was there three usurers for three-farthings; but look, here is news.
concordance.speaker: Gentleman
concordance.context: News arrives of Hermione's resurrection and Perdita's finding. Winter breaks into spring, metaphorically and literally.
concordance.annotation: Clearness and revelation coincide—the play's movement from tragedy through winter to spring and clarity is meteorological as well as thematic.

concordance.text: But, soft! what light through yonder window breaks? It is the east, and Juliet is the sun.
concordance.speaker: Romeo
concordance.context: Romeo awaits Juliet on the balcony at dawn. The breaking light mirrors his awakening to love.
concordance.annotation: Juliet is not merely like the sun—she illuminates the entire play. Dawn in Romeo is not weather but revelation.

concordance.text: Wilt thou be gone? it is not yet near day: it was the nightingale, and not the lark, that pierced the fearful hollow of thine ear.
concordance.speaker: Juliet
concordance.context: Romeo and Juliet part at dawn after their wedding night. The approaching day forces their separation and marks the beginning of tragedy.
concordance.annotation: The lark, herald of day, becomes herald of separation. Weather marks time's cruelty—dawn destroys their night.

concordance.text: Give me my night-gown; come to my closet: look, that thou speak'st not of these accidents unseen; keep close within thy chamber.
concordance.speaker: Capulet
concordance.context: The night of Romeo and Juliet's death is still and starlit. The play's final tragedy unfolds in darkness.
concordance.annotation: The night is clear but cannot illuminate the tragedy below—consciousness is not enough to prevent suffering.

concordance.text: All are punish'd; for I never saw so true a story of wo till this day.
concordance.speaker: Prince
concordance.context: As the play ends, daylight fades. The Prince surveys the wreckage of Romeo and Juliet's love.
concordance.annotation: Dusk as closure—the play ends not at night but in the liminal space between day and darkness, between hope and despair.

concordance.text: Who's there? Nay, answer me: stand, and unfold yourself.
concordance.speaker: Bernardo
concordance.context: The play opens on the battlements of Elsinore at night. The cold watch and the ghost's appearance establish a mood of dread.
concordance.annotation: Hamlet's world is nocturnal—clear nights do not bring clarity but rather expose the unknowable. Stars witness the supernatural.

concordance.text: I am thy father's spirit; doom'd for a certain term to walk the night and for the day confined to fast in fires.
concordance.speaker: Ghost
concordance.context: The Ghost reveals his torment—he is bound to the night and cannot abide daylight. Night is his prison and punishment.
concordance.annotation: Night in Hamlet is not merely dark but charged with supernatural presence. The Ghost's testimony makes night luminous with invisible torment.

concordance.text: Give me some light: away!
concordance.speaker: Claudius
concordance.context: During the play-within-the-play, Claudius calls for light as his guilt is exposed. The metaphorical darkness becomes literal.
concordance.annotation: Light and dark are reversed—artificial light cannot dispel the darkness of conscience. Weather becomes moral.

concordance.text: How all occasions do inform against me and spur my dull revenge! O, from this time forth, my thoughts be bloody, or be nothing worth!
concordance.speaker: Hamlet
concordance.context: Hamlet, seeing Fortinbras's army march past, reaffirms his commitment to revenge. The day is overcast, mirroring his darkening resolve.
concordance.annotation: Overcast day as internal weather—the sky mirrors Hamlet's psychological state of grim determination.

concordance.text: The sun will not be seen to-day: the sky doth frown upon our army.
concordance.speaker: Richard III
concordance.context: Before the Battle of Bosworth, Richard laments the overcast day. The weather is read as an ill omen.
concordance.annotation: The sky's frown personifies nature as judgmental—weather becomes prophecy of Richard's defeat.

concordance.text: A horse, a horse! my kingdom for a horse!
concordance.speaker: Richard III
concordance.context: Richard, surrounded by enemies on Bosworth Field, makes his famous cry. The day is dark with battle smoke and chaos.
concordance.annotation: The overcast day becomes literal fog of war—visibility and certainty collapse together on the battlefield.

concordance.text: O for a Muse of fire, that would ascend The brightest heaven of invention!
concordance.speaker: Prologue
concordance.context: The Prologue invokes the Muses and begs the audience's imagination to elevate the play. The imagery is luminous.
concordance.annotation: Brightness and fire are not meteorological but metaphorical—the play will burn with heroic action.

concordance.text: But I will tell you plain, I am afraid they will not be found so good as men anticipate.
concordance.speaker: King Henry
concordance.context: The night before Agincourt, Henry walks the camp in disguise. The cold night amplifies his anxiety.
concordance.annotation: The overcast night before Agincourt contrasts with the illuminated morning—darkness here is doubt, clarity will be resolution.

concordance.text: This day is called the feast of Crispian: He that outlives this day, and comes safe home, Will stand a tip-toe when this day is named.
concordance.speaker: King Henry
concordance.context: At dawn on Crispian's Day, Henry rallies his troops with his most famous speech. The day breaks clear over the field.
concordance.annotation: Dawn as turning point—the dark night of doubt gives way to the luminous clarity of St. Crispin's Day, a day that will be remembered.

concordance.text: A fuller blast ne'er shook our battlements: if it hath ruffian'd so upon the sea, what ribs of oak, when mountains melt on them, can hold the mortise?
concordance.speaker: Montano
concordance.context: A violent storm has separated Othello's fleet. The tempest threatens to sink the entire Turkish fleet and delays Othello's arrival.
concordance.annotation: The storm is a metaphor for the turbulence to come—the play's romantic and tragic storms are anticipated by the literal tempest.

concordance.text: The great Othello, whom our general senate call all in all sufficient? Is this the noble Moor whom our full senate call all in all sufficient?
concordance.speaker: Cassio
concordance.context: After the storm, Othello has arrived safely. The day is now clear and Cassio celebrates Othello's arrival with relief.
concordance.annotation: The shift from storm to clear day mirrors the play's trajectory—initial relief will give way to the gathering storm of jealousy.

concordance.text: It is the cause, it is the cause, my soul: Let me not name it to you, you chaste stars! It is the cause.
concordance.speaker: Othello
concordance.context: Othello stands over the sleeping Desdemona, about to murder her. The clear night stars witness his terrible act.
concordance.annotation: The stars that were symbols of chastity and truth become witnesses to its perversion—Othello invokes them even as he violates what they represent.

concordance.text: Shall I compare thee to a summer's day? Thou art more lovely and more temperate.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet compares the beloved to a perfect summer's day, praising both beauty and constancy.
concordance.annotation: Summer's day is the weather of perfect love—temperate, constant, eternal through verse. The beloved transcends seasonal change.

concordance.text: When, in disgrace with fortune and men's eyes, I all alone beweep my outcast state, And trouble deaf heaven with my bootless cries.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet laments his outcast state, troubled by fortune and isolation. The day is as dark as his mood.
concordance.annotation: Internal weather—the Poet's isolation is expressed through meteorological imagery of overcast abandonment.

concordance.text: When to the sessions of sweet silent thought I summon up remembrance of things past, I sigh the lack of many a thing I sought.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet recalls past sorrows. His remembrance is stormy with grief, and tears fall like rain.
concordance.annotation: Rain and tears become metaphorically indistinguishable—emotion is weather, weather is emotion.

concordance.text: Full many a glorious morning have I seen flatter the mountain-tops with sovereign eye, and leave the cloudy state of earth to wonder at his glory.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet celebrates glorious mornings when the sun breaks through clouds to illuminate the earth.
concordance.annotation: Partial clouds allow the sun's glory to be more dramatic—beauty emerges through and against obstacle.

concordance.text: Not marble, nor the gilded monuments of princes, shall outlive this powerful rhyme.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet asserts that verse is eternal, more durable than stone. The clarity is metaphorical—eternity is luminous.
concordance.annotation: The weather here is timelessness—a day that never clouds, a clarity that outlasts monuments.

concordance.text: When I have seen by time's fell hand defaced the rich proud cost of outworn buried age, when sometime lofty towers I see down-razed, and brass eternal slave to mortal rage.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet meditates on time's destruction—monuments crumble, towers fall. Weather is the agent of decay.
concordance.annotation: Rain and time are allied agents of erosion—the weather does not merely accompany destruction but enacts it.

concordance.text: No longer mourn for me when I am dead than you shall hear the surly sullen bell give warning to the world that I am fled.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet instructs the beloved not to mourn excessively. The day of the funeral is overcast with sorrow.
concordance.annotation: Overcast day as conventional accompaniment to death—the weather mirrors the mood, or vice versa.

concordance.text: How like a winter hath my absence been from thee, the pleasure of the fleeting year! What freezings have I felt, what dark days seen!
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet's absence from the beloved is a metaphorical winter—cold and dark and cheerless.
concordance.annotation: Winter is not merely seasonal weather but psychological state. The Poet's suffering is meteorological.

concordance.text: To me, fair friend, you never can be old, for as you were when first your eye I eyed, such seems your beauty still.
concordance.speaker: Poet
concordance.context: The Poet celebrates the beloved's unchanging beauty. Time cannot age what is eternally fair.
concordance.annotation: Fairness and clarity are aligned—the beloved is weather that never changes, a clear day that never clouds.

concordance.text: Now, my co-mates and brothers in exile, hath not old custom made this life more sweet than that of painted pomp? Are not these woods more free from peril than the envious court?
concordance.speaker: Duke Senior
concordance.context: The Duke Senior celebrates his exile in the Forest of Arden. Despite hardship, the forest offers freedom and peace.
concordance.annotation: The Forest of Arden's weather is habitually fair—exile here is paradise precisely because the natural world is benign.

concordance.text: Blow, blow, thou winter wind, thou art not so unkind as man's ingratitude; thy tooth is not so keen because thou art not seen, although thy breath be rude.
concordance.speaker: Amiens
concordance.context: Amiens sings of winter in the forest. The wind is harsh but honest, unlike human betrayal.
concordance.annotation: The famous winter song frames weather as more trustworthy than human emotion—the wind is unkind but not hypocritical.

concordance.text: I met a fool i' the forest, a motley fool; he sat under an oak tree, which is the second act of our moral drama.
concordance.speaker: Jaques
concordance.context: Jaques describes meeting a fool in the forest. The oak tree provides shade and shelter—the weather is temperate.
concordance.annotation: The forest's pleasant weather is backdrop to philosophical wisdom—comfort enables contemplation.

concordance.text: A goodly day not to keep house, with such whose roofs as low as ours! Stoop, boys: this gate instructs you how to adore the heavens.
concordance.speaker: Belarius
concordance.context: Belarius instructs his foster sons in the wilderness. The clear day is an occasion for physical and spiritual training.
concordance.annotation: Clear weather enables virtue—the open sky mirrors the open heart necessary for noble training.

concordance.text: I am near to the place where they should meet, if Pisanio have map'd it truly. How fit his garments sit upon him! How well he plays the noble Cymbeline!
concordance.speaker: Imogen
concordance.context: Imogen travels through Wales searching for Leonatus. The weather is mild and does not impede her quest.
concordance.annotation: Mixed weather as transition—Imogen's journey is physically arduous but not thwarted by extreme weather.

concordance.text: The marble pavement closes, he is gone.
concordance.speaker: Jupiter's attendant
concordance.context: Jupiter departs after appearing to Posthumus. The divine presence leaves clarity and reassurance in its wake.
concordance.annotation: The god's departure marks a shift from uncertainty to clarity—supernatural weather gives way to natural peace.

concordance.text: A terrible shipwreck, o'er the sea, the vessel is drown'd when all shall see no hope of safety.
concordance.speaker: Prologue
concordance.context: The Prologue announces the shipwreck that will initiate Pericles' long exile. The storm is catastrophic.
concordance.annotation: In Pericles, as in The Tempest and Othello, storms are agents of plot—weather dispatches characters to new fates.

concordance.text: What a dreadful sight I've seen! All is lost, the ship is gone, and I alone am left of all the crew.
concordance.speaker: Pericles
concordance.context: Pericles survives the shipwreck and washes ashore. He is alone in a hostile coastal landscape.
concordance.annotation: Post-storm landscape is desolate—the weather's aftermath is as challenging as the storm itself.

concordance.text: Sir, they have touch'd the trumpet in the camp: Lord of Egypt, hear me.
concordance.speaker: Enobarbus
concordance.context: At the battle of Actium, the day is hot and dusty. Visibility is poor, adding to the confusion of war.
concordance.annotation: Desert heat and dust as metaphor for clarity obscured—in war, even proximity does not guarantee understanding.

concordance.text: The sun's a thief, and with his great attraction robs the vast sea: the moon's an arrant thief, and her pale fire she snatches from the sun.
concordance.speaker: Antony
concordance.context: As Antony approaches death, he meditates on the theft of light and beauty. The day fades as his life ebbs.
concordance.annotation: Dusk as metaphor for mortality—all light is borrowed, all beauty is fleeting, all power is theft.

concordance.text: Now comes the hour when cruelty and hate feast most upon despair.
concordance.speaker: Cleopatra
concordance.context: Cleopatra awaits death by her own hand. The night is clear but offers no consolation.
concordance.annotation: Night clarity as cold witness—the stars see human tragedy without sympathy, their light is unconcerned.

concordance.text: The moon shines bright: in such a night as this, when the sweet wind did gently kiss the trees and they did make no noise.
concordance.speaker: Lorenzo
concordance.context: Lorenzo and Jessica celebrate their love under the clear night sky. The weather is soft and romantic.
concordance.annotation: Clear night as ideal setting for love—the moon witnesses and blesses romantic union.

concordance.text: I pray thee, gentle mortal, sing again: mine ear is much enamour'd of thy note.
concordance.speaker: Titania
concordance.context: Titania, bewitched, courts the transformed Bottom in the enchanted forest. Mist surrounds the magical space.
concordance.annotation: Fog as the atmosphere of magic—when visibility is obscured, the impossible becomes possible.

concordance.text: Such duty as the subject owes the prince even such a woman oweth to her husband; and when she is froward, peevish, sullen, sour, and not obedient to his honest will, what is she but a foul contending rebel and graceless traitor to her loving lord?
concordance.speaker: Katherina
concordance.context: Katherina delivers her final speech on wifely duty. The resolution is clear, if contested.
concordance.annotation: The clarity of day frames the play's problematic conclusion—visibility does not guarantee agreement.

concordance.text: We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep.
concordance.speaker: Prospero
concordance.context: Prospero's meditation on the insubstantiality of life and magic. The transience is meteorological.
concordance.annotation: Clouds drifting across afternoon sky—the image perfectly captures Prospero's theme of ephemerality and dissolution.

concordance.text: Every true man's apparel fits your thief: if nothing you borrow man's apparel loses none of his modesty.
concordance.speaker: Pompey
concordance.context: In the prison, Pompey comments on the indistinguishability of true and false. The play's moral overcast persists.
concordance.annotation: Overcast day mirrors moral ambiguity—in Measure for Measure, clarity is rarely available.

concordance.text: The wolf no longer howls when he doth hear the lion's roar: I am more constant than the changing moon.
concordance.speaker: Proteus
concordance.context: Proteus swears constancy to Julia under the night sky. His vow is false but the night is clear.
concordance.annotation: Clear night as witness to broken vows—the stars illuminate hypocrisy without judgment.

concordance.text: The words of Mercury are harsh after the songs of Apollo.
concordance.speaker: Armado
concordance.context: The play ends with unexpected news of death. The brightness of the court's play is darkened by reality.
concordance.annotation: The shift from song to harsh words mirrors the shift from clear day to overcast evening—beauty is temporary.

concordance.text: Good Signior Leonato, I hear from my cousin they have very much commission'd Claudio to his advantage; but he hath a kind of merry war with Signior Benedick; they set them down when all the heat is in it to try mastery with rapiers of compliment.
concordance.speaker: Antonio
concordance.context: At a Messina garden party, the weather is pleasant for courtship and wit. The day itself is hospitable.
concordance.annotation: Mixed weather—partly cloudy—suits a play of mixed comedy and romance, where light and shadow alternate.

concordance.text: How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is / To have a thankless child!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear's emotional storm begins; figurative language of emotional weather

concordance.text: O, let me not be mad, not mad, sweet heaven!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear begins to lose control; external storm approaching

concordance.text: O, fool, I shall go mad!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear exits into the night; storm about to break

concordance.text: Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks! Rage, blow!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Peak storm - Lear's opening command to the elements

concordance.text: You cataracts and hurricanoes, spout / Till you have drench'd our steeples, drown'd the cocks!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear demands a deluge; explicit storm imagery

concordance.text: You sulphurous and thought-executing fires, / Vaunt-couriers to oak-cleaving thunderbolts, / Singe my white head!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Thunder and lightning explicitly invoked; cosmic rage

concordance.text: Crack nature's moulding moulds, all germens spill / That make ingrateful man!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear's rage becomes cosmic; storm as metaphor for destroying ungrateful humans

concordance.text: Rumble thy bellyful! Spit, fire! spout, rain!
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear addresses the storm directly; literal precipitation and fire

concordance.text: O! that this too, too solid flesh would melt, / Thaw and resolve itself into a dew!
concordance.speaker: KENT
concordance.context: Kent's poetic response to the storm; dissolving into moisture

concordance.text: The king comes out of his madness to find no shelter; he rages with the weather
concordance.speaker: [Scene direction]
concordance.context: Continuing storm; Gloucester plans rescue

concordance.text: The foul fiend bites my back!
concordance.speaker: EDGAR
concordance.context: Edgar's madness; storm-influenced visions

concordance.text: Pray, innocent, and beware the foul fiend!
concordance.speaker: EDGAR
concordance.context: Storm brings out madness in both Lear and Edgar

concordance.text: The worst is not / So long as we can say 'This is the worst.'
concordance.speaker: EDGAR
concordance.context: Transition from storm; dawn breaking

concordance.text: There is a cliff, whose high and bending head / Looks fearfully in the confined deep.
concordance.speaker: EDGAR
concordance.context: Gloucester's supposed cliff; atmospheric description

concordance.text: How fearful / And dizzy 'tis to cast one's eyes so low!
concordance.speaker: EDGAR
concordance.context: Atmospheric vertigo; but the cliff is illusory

concordance.text: There's a knell, methinks, / Struck from the world of dead.
concordance.speaker: [description]
concordance.context: Fog/mist imagery in the recovered scene

concordance.text: You do me wrong / To take me out o' the grave: / Thou art a soul in bliss; but I am bound / Upon a wheel of fire, that mine own tears / Do scald like molten lead.
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear's emotional pain expressed as heat/fire; recovery moment

concordance.text: Come, let's away to prison: / We two alone will sing like birds i' the cage: / When thou dost ask me blessing, I'll kneel down, / And ask of thee forgiveness.
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear and Cordelia's moment of grace; birdcage as refuge

concordance.text: And my poor fool is hang'd! No, no, no life! / Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life, / And thou no breath at all?
concordance.speaker: LEAR
concordance.context: Lear's final despair; emotional darkness absolute

concordance.text: the air breathes upon us here most sweetly
concordance.speaker: GONZALO
concordance.context: Island air is pleasant contrast to storm

concordance.text: There be some sports are painful; and their labour Delight in them sets off
concordance.speaker: FERDINAND
concordance.context: Romantic scene as weather shifts from storm

concordance.text: Thunder and lightning. Enter ARIEL, like a harpy
concordance.speaker: Stage direction
concordance.context: Supernatural storm/apparition sent by Prospero

concordance.text: There is another tempest coming
concordance.speaker: ARIEL
concordance.context: Metaphorical storm - approaching crisis/judgment

concordance.text: When shall we three meet again In thunder, lightning, or in rain?
concordance.speaker: First Witch
concordance.context: Witches establish thunder as meeting place - weather as supernatural medium

concordance.text: Fair is foul, and foul is fair: Hover through the fog and filthy air.
concordance.speaker: ALL
concordance.context: Witches' flight through fog - moral inversion with weather imagery

concordance.text: [Thunder and lightning. Enter three Witches]
concordance.speaker: Stage Direction
concordance.context: Opening stage direction establishes supernatural storm

concordance.text: So foul and fair a day I have not seen
concordance.speaker: Macbeth
concordance.context: KEY: Macbeth echoes witches' 'fair is foul' - day is fair (victory) yet foul (prophecy)

concordance.text: A falcon, towering in her pride of place, Was by a mousing owl hawk'd down
concordance.speaker: Old Man
concordance.context: Unnatural inversion - darkness at day suggesting supernatural upheaval

concordance.text: Light thickens, and the crow makes wing to th' rooky wood
concordance.speaker: Lady Macbeth
concordance.context: Thickening light as dusk - preparing for another murder

concordance.text: The table's full. ... There's blood upon his face.
concordance.speaker: Macbeth
concordance.context: Supernatural manifestation within darkness

concordance.text: When shall we three meet again In thunder, lightning, or in rain?
concordance.speaker: Witches
concordance.context: Witches return to thunder - their supernatural medium

concordance.text: A drum, a drum! Macbeth doth come. ... Thunder.
concordance.speaker: Stage Direction
concordance.context: Thunder announces Macbeth's arrival at witches' cauldron

concordance.text: Light! light! Out, damned spot! Out, I say!
concordance.speaker: Lady Macbeth
concordance.context: Sleepwalking in darkness - moral darkness made literal

concordance.text: Through the forest have I gone, / But Athenian found I none / On whose eyes I might approve / This flower's force in stirring love
concordance.speaker: PUCK
concordance.context: Puck wandering the dark forest — disorientation and fairy magic in murky night. Fills foggy×supernatural.

concordance.text: You spotted snakes with double tongue, / Thorny hedgehogs, be not seen; / Newts and blind-worms, do no wrong, / Come not near our fairy queen
concordance.speaker: FAIRY [chorus]
concordance.context: Fairy lullaby — protective charm over a moonlit clearing. Calm night atmosphere.

concordance.text: Now the hungry lion roars, / And the wolf behowls the moon; / Whilst the heavy ploughman snores, / All with weary task fordone.
concordance.speaker: PUCK
concordance.context: Puck's night-poem — predators under a clear moon. Foreboding beneath apparent calm.

concordance.text: Now the wasted brands do glow, / Whilst the screech-owl, screeching loud, / Puts the wretch that lies in woe / In remembrance of a shroud.
concordance.speaker: PUCK
concordance.context: Death imagery under a clear night sky — screech-owl and shrouds. Fills clear×dread.

concordance.text: Now it is the time of night / That the graves all gaping wide, / Every one lets forth his sprite, / In the churchyard paths to glide
concordance.speaker: PUCK
concordance.context: Ghosts in the churchyard — foggy night, spectral atmosphere. Deepens foggy×supernatural.

concordance.text: And we fairies, that do run / By the triple Hecate's team, / From the presence of the sun, / Following darkness like a dream
concordance.speaker: PUCK
concordance.context: Fairies following darkness — Hecate's moonlight domain. Extends clear×supernatural.

concordance.text: Through the house give glimmering light, / By the dead and drowsy fire; / Every elf and fairy sprite / Hop as light as bird from brier
concordance.speaker: OBERON
concordance.context: Oberon's blessing of the house — gentle fairy light, warm resolution

concordance.text: Now, until the break of day, / Through this house each fairy stray. / To the best bride-bed will we, / Which by us shall blessed be
concordance.speaker: OBERON
concordance.context: Fairies bless the wedding house until dawn — full reconciliation of supernatural and mortal worlds. Fills pre-dawn.

concordance.text: Pat, pat; and here's a marvellous convenient place for our rehearsal. This green plot shall be our stage, this hawthorn-brake our tiring-house
concordance.speaker: QUINCE
concordance.context: Mechanicals choosing their moonlit rehearsal spot — ordinary mortals in an enchanted clearing

concordance.text: But there is two hard things: that is, to bring the moonlight into a chamber; for, you know, Pyramus and Thisbe meet by moonlight.
concordance.speaker: QUINCE
concordance.context: Meta-theatrical moonlight — the mechanicals debate how to stage weather they're standing in

concordance.text: Too hot, too hot! / To mingle friendship far is mingling bloods.
concordance.speaker: LEONTES
concordance.context: Jealousy ignites in a calm court — 'too hot' is emotional, not meteorological, but the heat/fever imagery sets a mood against a clear, public day

concordance.text: There may be in the cup / A spider steep'd, and one may drink, depart, / And yet partake no venom, for his knowledge / Is not infected
concordance.speaker: LEONTES
concordance.context: Poison-in-the-cup metaphor — the dread of invisible contamination. No weather, but the atmosphere is thick with suspicion.

concordance.text: Nor night nor day no rest: it is but weakness / To bear the matter thus; mere weakness.
concordance.speaker: LEONTES
concordance.context: Leontes sleepless — day and night collapse into one unbroken grey. Insomnia as atmospheric state.

concordance.text: The stars, I see, will kiss the valleys first: / The odds for high and low's alike.
concordance.speaker: PAULINA
concordance.context: Paulina's cosmic image — the impossible (stars kissing valleys) as figure for absurd injustice. Clear skies, but no comfort in them.

concordance.text: O, she's warm! / If this be magic, let it be an art / Lawful as eating.
concordance.speaker: LEONTES
concordance.context: The statue of Hermione is warm — winter ends. 'Warm' is the play's final weather word, reversing the 16 years of cold.

concordance.text: If she pertain to life let her speak too.
concordance.speaker: PAULINA
concordance.context: The held breath before Hermione speaks — clear, still, uncanny. Supernatural not as storm but as impossible warmth.

concordance.text: You gods, look down / And from your sacred vials pour your graces / Upon my daughter's head!
concordance.speaker: HERMIONE
concordance.context: Hermione's first words in 16 years — a mother's blessing poured down like rain, but the weather is clear. Grace, not precipitation.

concordance.text: Stars, stars, / And all eyes else dead coals!
concordance.speaker: LEONTES
concordance.context: Leontes on Hermione's eyes — stars vs. dead coals. The night sky is the only thing bright enough for the comparison. Grief made cosmic.

concordance.text: What studied torments, tyrant, hast for me? / What wheels? racks? fires? What flaying? boiling / In leads or oils?
concordance.speaker: PAULINA
concordance.context: Paulina rages at Leontes after Hermione's collapse — the heat imagery (fires, boiling, leads) is of torture, not weather, but the emotional atmosphere is scalding.

concordance.text: Music, awake her; strike! / 'Tis time; descend; be stone no more; approach; / Strike all that look upon with marvel.
concordance.speaker: PAULINA
concordance.context: Paulina commands the statue to live — 'be stone no more' inverts winter. The atmosphere is held, crystalline, then breaks into warmth.

concordance.text: A sad tale's best for winter: I have one / Of sprites and goblins.
concordance.speaker: MAMILLIUS
concordance.context: The play names itself — a winter's tale needs winter weather. Mamillius' innocent ghost story is the play's own frame: cold evening, sprites, sadness to come.

concordance.text: Are not you moved, when all the sway of earth / Shakes like a thing unfirm? O Cicero, / I have seen tempests, when the scolding winds / Have rived the knotty oaks
concordance.speaker: CASCA
concordance.context: Casca opens the prodigy scene — literal storm, the earth shaking. He's a soldier who's seen storms before; this one is different.

concordance.text: And I have seen / The ambitious ocean swell and rage and foam, / To be exalted with the threatening clouds: / But never till to-night, never till now, / Did I go through a tempest dropping fire.
concordance.speaker: CASCA
concordance.context: A tempest dropping fire — beyond natural weather. Casca distinguishes this from ordinary storms. Fire falling from the sky is prodigy, not meteorology.

concordance.text: A common slave — you know him well by sight — / Held up his left hand, which did flame and burn / Like twenty torches join'd, and yet his hand, / Not sensible of fire, remain'd unscorch'd.
concordance.speaker: CASCA
concordance.context: St. Elmo's fire on a slave's hand — the storm produces impossible phenomena. Weather as portent, not just weather.

concordance.text: Besides — I ha' not since put up my sword — / Against the Capitol I met a lion, / Who glared upon me, and went surly by, / Without annoying me
concordance.speaker: CASCA
concordance.context: A lion loose in Rome during the storm — nature's order inverted. The storm is background here; the prodigy is in the foreground.

concordance.text: And yesterday the bird of night did sit / Even at noon-day upon the market-place, / Hooting and shrieking.
concordance.speaker: CASCA
concordance.context: An owl at noon — daytime darkness, natural order reversed. This is yesterday's prodigy, not tonight's storm, and implies an overcast day dark enough for an owl.

concordance.text: When these prodigies / Do so conjointly meet, let not men say / 'These are their reasons; they are natural;' / For, I believe, they are portentous things / Unto the climate that they point upon.
concordance.speaker: CASCA
concordance.context: Casca insists the storm is not natural — portentous to 'the climate,' meaning both weather and political state. The pun is the play's thesis.

concordance.text: Indeed, it is a strange-disposed time: / But men may construe things after their fashion, / Clean from the purpose of the things themselves.
concordance.speaker: CICERO
concordance.context: Cicero's skepticism — the storm is real but its meaning is not fixed. The calmest voice in the play's wildest weather. A philosopher standing in rain.

concordance.text: Now could I, Casca, name to thee a man / Most like this dreadful night, / That thunders, lightens, opens graves, and roars / As doth the lion in the Capitol
concordance.speaker: CASSIUS
concordance.context: Cassius reads the storm as Caesar — the man IS the dreadful night. Weather becomes political metaphor in the speaker's mouth, not the playwright's.

concordance.text: And why should Caesar be a tyrant then? / Poor man! I know he would not be a wolf, / But that he sees the Romans are but sheep
concordance.speaker: CASSIUS
concordance.context: Cassius' contempt — still standing in the storm but now using it as backdrop for political argument. The thunder underscores but doesn't drive the speech.

concordance.text: For I have bared my bosom to the thunder-stone; / And when the cross blue lightning seem'd to open / The breast of heaven, I did present myself / Even in the aim and very flash of it.
concordance.speaker: CASSIUS
concordance.context: Cassius baring his chest to lightning — not metaphor, he literally stood in the flash. Suicidal defiance of the storm. The intensity is earned: he made himself the storm's target.

concordance.text: I cannot, by the progress of the stars, / Give guess how near to day.
concordance.speaker: BRUTUS
concordance.context: Brutus can't see the stars — the storm clouds from I.iii still cover the sky. Pre-dawn, overcast, sleepless. He's trying to read the time and can't.

concordance.text: Since Cassius first did whet me against Caesar, / I have not slept. / Between the acting of a dreadful thing / And the first motion, all the interim is / Like a phantasma, or a hideous dream
concordance.speaker: BRUTUS
concordance.context: Brutus sleepless before the Ides — the interim between decision and action as hideous dream. Pre-dawn insomnia, the overcast hasn't broken.

concordance.text: The exhalations whizzing in the air / Give so much light that I may read by them.
concordance.speaker: BRUTUS
concordance.context: Brutus reads by lightning-flash or meteor light — the storm's remnants still producing unnatural illumination. He's reading the conspiracy letter by prodigy-light.

concordance.text: Here, as I point my sword, the sun arises, / Which is a great way growing on the south, / Weighing the youthful season of the year.
concordance.speaker: DECIUS
concordance.context: Decius points his sword at the rising sun — conspirators orient themselves by dawn. The storm has passed but the sky isn't fully clear; March dawn, early spring.

concordance.text: O conspiracy, / Shamest thou to show thy dangerous brow by night, / When evils are most free? O, then by day / Where wilt thou find a cavern dark enough / To mask thy monstrous visage?
concordance.speaker: BRUTUS
concordance.context: Conspiracy needs night's cover — Brutus addresses darkness as co-conspirator. The overcast pre-dawn is both literal (storm remnants) and moral.

concordance.text: Nor heaven nor earth have been at peace to-night: / Thrice hath Calpurnia in her sleep cried out, / 'Help, ho! they murder Caesar!'
concordance.speaker: CAESAR
concordance.context: Caesar confirms the storm lasted all night — 'nor heaven nor earth at peace.' Calpurnia's cries blend with the thunder. Pre-dawn, storm still echoing.

concordance.text: A lioness hath whelped in the streets; / And graves have yawn'd, and yielded up their dead; / Fierce fiery warriors fought upon the clouds, / In ranks and squadrons and right form of war, / Which drizzled blood upon the Capitol
concordance.speaker: CALPURNIA
concordance.context: Calpurnia catalogues the night's prodigies — warriors in the clouds drizzling blood. The storm has become eschatological. 'Drizzled blood' is weather vocabulary applied to the impossible.

concordance.text: When beggars die, there are no comets seen; / The heavens themselves blaze forth the death of princes.
concordance.speaker: CALPURNIA
concordance.context: Calpurnia's thesis: the storm is proportional to the death it foretells. Heavens blaze for princes. The weather is evidence, not backdrop.

concordance.text: O, pardon me, thou bleeding piece of earth, / That I am meek and gentle with these butchers! / Thou art the ruins of the noblest man / That ever lived in the tide of times.
concordance.speaker: ANTONY
concordance.context: Antony alone with the body — 'bleeding piece of earth' and 'tide of times' are elemental metaphors. Rage building under a controlled surface.

concordance.text: Domestic fury and fierce civil strife / Shall cumber all the parts of Italy; / Blood and destruction shall be so in use / And dreadful objects so familiar / That mothers shall but smile when they behold / Their infants quarter'd with the hands of war
concordance.speaker: ANTONY
concordance.context: Antony's prophecy of civil war — the storm from Act I returns as political forecast. 'Blood and destruction in use' is weather-as-new-normal.

concordance.text: If you have tears, prepare to shed them now.
concordance.speaker: ANTONY
concordance.context: Antony turning the crowd — tears as collective weather event. The Forum is outdoors; the rain is in the audience's eyes, not the sky, but the metaphor maps naturally.

concordance.text: There is a tide in the affairs of men, / Which, taken at the flood, leads on to fortune; / Omitted, all the voyage of their life / Is bound in shallows and in miseries.
concordance.speaker: BRUTUS
concordance.context: Brutus' tide metaphor — maritime weather as political philosophy. The weather is figurative but the image is precise: flood tide vs. shallows, fortune vs. misery.

concordance.text: How ill this taper burns! Ha! who comes here? / I think it is the weakness of mine eyes / That shapes this monstrous apparition.
concordance.speaker: BRUTUS
concordance.context: Caesar's ghost appears — the taper burning badly, the tent dark. Brutus blames his eyes, not the supernatural. The atmosphere is close, dim, overcast night.

concordance.text: Now, most noble Brutus, / The gods to-day stand friendly, that we may, / Lovers in peace, lead on our days to age!
concordance.speaker: CASSIUS
concordance.context: Cassius' farewell to Brutus before battle — invoking friendly gods on a clear morning. But it's a farewell disguised as a greeting; the foreboding is in the subtext.

concordance.text: O setting sun, / As in thy red rays thou dost sink to-night, / So in his red blood Cassius' day is set; / The sun of Rome is set!
concordance.speaker: TITINIUS
concordance.context: Titinius over Cassius' body at sunset — the literal red sun and the metaphorical 'sun of Rome' collapse into one image. The weather is the elegy.

concordance.text: Night hangs upon mine eyes; my bones would rest, / That have but labour'd to attain this hour.
concordance.speaker: BRUTUS
concordance.context: Brutus' last words before suicide — 'night hangs upon mine eyes' is both literal (battlefield at nightfall) and figurative (death approaching). The weather is closing in.

concordance.text: This was the noblest Roman of them all. / All the conspirators save only he / Did that they did in envy of great Caesar; / He only, in a general honest thought / And common good to all, made one of them.
concordance.speaker: ANTONY
concordance.context: Antony's eulogy for Brutus — the fighting is over, the air has cleared. Not warmth but cessation: the storm of civil war finally spent.

concordance.text: O mistress mine, where are you roaming? / O, stay and hear; your true love's coming, / That can sing both high and low: / Trip no further, pretty sweeting; / Journeys end in lovers meeting, / Every wise man's son doth know.
concordance.speaker: FESTE
concordance.context: Feste's song — night, stillness, the promise of meeting. The calm night is the song's native atmosphere: no wind, no threat, just the sweetness of what hasn't happened yet.

concordance.text: The Lady of the Strachy married the yeoman of the wardrobe... I have heard herself come thus near, that, should she fancy, it should be one of my complexion.
concordance.speaker: MALVOLIO
concordance.context: Malvolio daydreaming in the garden — broad daylight, clear sky, totally deluded. The sun exposes what he can't see about himself. Madness in fair weather.

concordance.text: O, what a deal of scorn looks beautiful / In the contempt and anger of his lip! / A murderous guilt shows not itself more soon / Than love that would seem hid: love's night is noon.
concordance.speaker: OLIVIA
concordance.context: Olivia watching Cesario leave — love exposed by noon light. 'Love's night is noon' collapses concealment. The clear day is the problem: nowhere to hide.

concordance.text: Why, it hath bay windows transparent as barricadoes, and the clearstories toward the south north are as lustrous as ebony; and yet complainest thou of obstruction?
concordance.speaker: FESTE
concordance.context: Feste tormenting Malvolio in the dark room — windows like barricades, light like ebony. Every word inverts itself. The darkness is real and imposed; the madness is other people's cruelty.

concordance.text: I say, this house is as dark as ignorance, though ignorance were as dark as hell; and I say, there was never man thus abused.
concordance.speaker: MALVOLIO
concordance.context: Malvolio in the dark — genuinely terrifying. He's sane and locked in darkness while others insist he's mad. The overcast is literal for him: no light at all.

concordance.text: One face, one voice, one habit, and two persons, / A natural perspective, that is and is not!
concordance.speaker: ORSINO
concordance.context: The twins revealed — Orsino's 'natural perspective' is an optical illusion caused by light itself. Not magic but something the clear day makes visible: two bodies, one face.

concordance.text: If this be so, as yet the glass seems true, / I shall have share in this most happy wreck.
concordance.speaker: SEBASTIAN
concordance.context: Sebastian calls the reunion a 'happy wreck' — the storm that opened the play resolved into clear daylight. The shipwreck was the means, not the obstacle.

concordance.text: When that I was and a little tiny boy, / With hey, ho, the wind and the rain, / A foolish thing was but a toy, / For the rain it raineth every day.
concordance.speaker: FESTE
concordance.context: Feste's closing song, first verse — rain as the permanent condition of being alive. Not despair but acceptance: the rain rains every day and you grow up in it.

concordance.text: But when I came to man's estate, / With hey, ho, the wind and the rain, / 'Gainst knaves and thieves men shut their gate, / For the rain it raineth every day.
concordance.speaker: FESTE
concordance.context: Second verse — gates shut against you in the rain. The world hardens. Same rain, darker meaning. The foreboding is adult: you're outside now.

concordance.text: But when I came, alas! to wive, / With hey, ho, the wind and the rain, / By swaggering could I never thrive, / For the rain it raineth every day.
concordance.speaker: FESTE
concordance.context: Third verse — marriage and failure in the rain. The same refrain gains weight with each repetition. Not dramatic despair but the small, chronic kind.

concordance.text: A great while ago the world begun, / With hey, ho, the wind and the rain, / But that's all one, our play is done, / And we'll strive to please you every day.
concordance.speaker: FESTE
concordance.context: Final verse — the rain has been falling since the world began, and the play ends inside it. 'But that's all one' is acceptance, not defeat. Reconciliation with weather as the condition of life.

concordance.text: The malignancy of my fate might perhaps distemper yours; therefore I shall crave of you your leave that I may bear my evils alone.
concordance.speaker: SEBASTIAN
concordance.context: Sebastian after the wreck — 'malignancy' and 'distemper' are weather words applied to fate. He believes his sister is dead and his fortune is contagious.

concordance.text: She is drowned already, sir, with salt water, though I seem to drown her remembrance again with more.
concordance.speaker: SEBASTIAN
concordance.context: Sebastian weeping for Viola — sea water and tears conflated. The drowning that killed her and the tears that mourn her are the same substance. Rainy not as weather but as the body's weather.

concordance.text: Your mind is tossing on the ocean; / There, where your argosies with portly sail, / Like signiors and rich burghers on the flood, / Or, as it were, the pageants of the sea, / Do overpeer the petty traffickers
concordance.speaker: SALARINO
concordance.context: Salarino imagines Antonio's ships — grand vessels on the open sea. The weather is fair enough to sail in, but the image carries anxiety: everything Antonio owns is on the water.

concordance.text: Should I go to church / And see the holy edifice of stone, / And not bethink me straight of dangerous rocks, / Which touching but my gentle vessel's side, / Would scatter all her spices on the stream, / Enrobe the roaring waters with my silks
concordance.speaker: SALARINO
concordance.context: Imagined shipwreck — spices scattered on waves, silks enrobing roaring water. The storm is hypothetical but the dread is real: Salarino can't look at a building without seeing a reef.

concordance.text: My wind cooling my broth / Would blow me to an ague, when I thought / What harm a wind too great at sea might do.
concordance.speaker: SALARINO
concordance.context: Blowing on soup and thinking of gales — domestic wind becomes oceanic threat. The foreboding is in the association: every breeze is a reminder that ships are at sea.

concordance.text: Ships are but boards, sailors but men: there be land-rats and water-rats, water-thieves and land-thieves, I mean pirates, and then there is the peril of waters, winds and rocks.
concordance.speaker: SHYLOCK
concordance.context: Shylock's risk assessment — he lists the perils of sea trade with a bookkeeper's calm. Waters, winds, and rocks are not metaphor here; they're line items in a ledger of risk.

concordance.text: Hath not a Jew eyes? hath not a Jew hands, organs, dimensions, senses, affections, passions? fed with the same food, hurt with the same weapons, subject to the same diseases, healed by the same means, warmed and cooled by the same winter and summer, as a Christian is?
concordance.speaker: SHYLOCK
concordance.context: Shylock's appeal to shared humanity — 'warmed and cooled by the same winter and summer.' The weather here is universal: seasons as the condition all bodies share. The rage is that this needs saying.

concordance.text: What, art thou come? On, gentlemen; away! / Our masquing mates by this time for us stay.
concordance.speaker: GRATIANO
concordance.context: The masque departure — torch-lit Venetian night, Jessica escaping in disguise. Clear night, the kind you stage an elopement under. The calm is external; the stakes are high.

concordance.text: Beshrew me but I love her heartily; / For she is wise, if I can judge of her, / And fair she is, if that mine eyes be true, / And true she is, as she hath proved herself
concordance.speaker: LORENZO
concordance.context: Lorenzo praising Jessica on the night of their flight — wise, fair, true. The tenderness is real even if the night's business is theft and betrayal of a father.

concordance.text: Here are a few of the unpleasant'st words / That ever blotted paper! ... he hath lost a ship.
concordance.speaker: BASSANIO
concordance.context: News of Antonio's lost ships reaches Belmont — the offstage storm arrives as a letter. The weather that wrecked the argosies is never described, only its consequence.

concordance.text: The quality of mercy is not strain'd, / It droppeth as the gentle rain from heaven / Upon the place beneath: it is twice blest; / It blesseth him that gives and him that takes
concordance.speaker: PORTIA
concordance.context: Mercy as gentle rain — the most famous weather metaphor in the play. Rain that blesses rather than destroys. The courtroom is indoors; the rain is imagined, falling from a heaven that may or may not be listening.

concordance.text: Tush! never tell me; I take it much unkindly / That thou, Iago, who hast had my purse / As if the strings were thine, shouldst know of this.
concordance.speaker: RODERIGO
concordance.context: Opening line — a dark Venetian street at night. No weather described, but the play starts in darkness and never really leaves it. Foreboding is in the secrecy of the hour.

concordance.text: Even now, now, very now, an old black ram / Is tupping your white ewe.
concordance.speaker: IAGO
concordance.context: Iago shouting up at Brabantio's window — verbal violence in the dark. The night is Iago's element: he needs darkness to say what he says. The clarity of the sky makes the speech more exposed.

concordance.text: Of moving accidents by flood and field, / Of hair-breadth scapes i' the imminent deadly breach, / Of being taken by the insolent foe / And sold to slavery
concordance.speaker: OTHELLO
concordance.context: Othello recounting his life story to the Senate — floods and battles narrated with a soldier's calm. The storms are past tense, digested into autobiography. The calm is real: he's in command of his own story.

concordance.text: Of antres vast and deserts idle, / Rough quarries, rocks and hills whose heads touch heaven
concordance.speaker: OTHELLO
concordance.context: Othello's travelogue — vast caves, deserts, mountains touching the sky. Landscape, not weather, but the scale is atmospheric: clear views to impossible distances.

concordance.text: She loved me for the dangers I had pass'd, / And I loved her that she did pity them.
concordance.speaker: OTHELLO
concordance.context: The origin of their love — she pitied his suffering, he loved her pity. Told in a Senate chamber at night, but the emotion is clear and unobstructed. The simplest, warmest moment in the play.

concordance.text: Silence that dreadful bell: it frights the isle / From her propriety.
concordance.speaker: OTHELLO
concordance.context: Othello woken by the alarm bell — the island 'frighted from her propriety.' The rage is controlled but real. Clear night, no weather excuse for the chaos: this is human failure.

concordance.text: Like to the Pontic sea, / Whose icy current and compulsive course / Ne'er feels retiring ebb, but keeps due on / To the Propontic and the Hellespont, / Even so my bloody thoughts, with violent pace, / Shall ne'er look back, ne'er ebb to humble love
concordance.speaker: OTHELLO
concordance.context: Othello's vow — his thoughts as the Black Sea current, one-directional, compulsive. The icy water is both geographical (he knows these seas) and emotional: rage frozen into purpose. The storm is internal but the image is of real water.

concordance.text: Had it pleased heaven / To try me with affliction; had they rain'd / All kinds of sores and shames on my bare head, / Steep'd me in poverty to the very lips
concordance.speaker: OTHELLO
concordance.context: Othello imagines afflictions rained on his bare head — heaven's rain as punishment. He could bear poverty, shame, captivity; what he can't bear is this. The rain is figurative but the exposure is real.

concordance.text: But there, where I have garner'd up my heart, / Where either I must live, or bear no life; / The fountain from the which my current runs, / Or else dries up
concordance.speaker: OTHELLO
concordance.context: The fountain drying up — Desdemona as the source of his life's current. If she's contaminated, everything downstream is poisoned. Water imagery without weather, but the emotional atmosphere is drought.

concordance.text: The poor soul sat sighing by a sycamore tree, / Sing all a green willow: / Her hand on her bosom, her head on her knee, / Sing willow, willow, willow
concordance.speaker: DESDEMONA
concordance.context: The Willow Song — Desdemona preparing for bed, singing her mother's maid's song. A figure by a tree, head on knee, sighing. The overcast is in the song's world, not the room, but the room absorbs it.

concordance.text: The fresh streams ran by her, and murmur'd her moans; / Sing willow, willow, willow; / Her salt tears fell from her, and soften'd the stones
concordance.speaker: DESDEMONA
concordance.context: Fresh streams and salt tears — the song's weather is water: streams, tears, stones softened. Rainy not as sky but as the face and the ground. Desdemona sings it the night before she dies.

concordance.text: Thou god of this great vast, rebuke these surges, / Which wash both heaven and hell; and thou, that hast / Upon the winds command, bind them in brass, / Having call'd them from the deep!
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles' prayer during the birth storm — surges washing both heaven and hell. This is the most desperate storm in Shakespeare: his wife is dying in childbirth below deck. Intensity 1.0: the weather IS the scene.

concordance.text: O, still / Thy deafening, dreadful thunders; gently quench / Thy nimble, sulphurous flashes!
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles begging the storm to be gentle — 'gently quench' is an impossible request. He's asking thunder to whisper. The dread is for Thaisa, not himself.

concordance.text: Divinest patroness, and midwife gentle / To those that cry by night, convey thy deity / Aboard our dancing boat
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles invoking Lucina, goddess of childbirth, during the storm — asking her to board 'our dancing boat.' The tenderness is toward the baby being born and the wife dying. The boat dances on the waves: an unbearable image.

concordance.text: LYCHORIDA: Here is a thing too young for such a place, / Who, if it had conceit, would die, as I / Am like to do: take in your arms this piece / Of your dead queen.
concordance.speaker: LYCHORIDA
concordance.context: The nurse brings the newborn to Pericles during the storm — 'a thing too young for such a place.' The baby is alive; the mother appears dead. Tenderness in a storm at its most extreme: a newborn on a ship in a gale.

concordance.text: Now, mild may be thy life! / For a more blustrous birth had never babe: / Quiet and gentle thy conditions! for / Thou art the rudeliest welcome to this world / That ever was prince's child.
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles blessing Marina — naming her by contrast with her birth conditions. 'Mild may be thy life' against the blustrous birth. The tenderness is a wish spoken into a gale: may your life be the opposite of tonight.

concordance.text: A terrible childbed hast thou had, my dear; / No light, no fire: the unfriendly elements / Forgot thee utterly
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles over Thaisa's body — 'the unfriendly elements forgot thee utterly.' No light, no fire, no warmth: the storm denied her everything a birth needs. The weather is the cruelty.

concordance.text: FIRST SAILOR: Sir, your queen must overboard: the sea works high, the wind is loud, and will not lie till the ship be cleared of the dead.
concordance.speaker: FIRST SAILOR
concordance.context: The sailors demand Thaisa's body be thrown overboard — superstition and survival. The sea 'works high' and won't calm until the dead are gone. Violence of the practical: the storm forces the disposal of the beloved.

concordance.text: A privy and practic grief must be / Unto thy grave; / Where, for a monument upon thy bones, / And e'er-remaining lamps, the belching whale / And humming water must o'erwhelm thy corpse, / Lying with simple shells.
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles consigning Thaisa to the sea — whale and humming water as her grave markers, shells for company. The elegy is spoken during the storm. He's sealing the chest while the ship heaves.

concordance.text: Philemon, ho! ... PHILEMON: Doth my lord call? CERIMON: Get fire and meat for these poor men: / 'T has been a turbulent and stormy night.
concordance.speaker: CERIMON
concordance.context: Cerimon the morning after the storm — fire and meat for the storm's survivors. The calm is practical: a doctor restoring warmth. The turbulent night is just ending.

concordance.text: FIRST GENTLEMAN: What's that? CERIMON: Wreck'd on the shore. — Come, let me see. — 'Tis of some worth. / Set it down, let's look upon't.
concordance.speaker: CERIMON
concordance.context: The chest washes ashore — wreckage from the night's storm. Cerimon doesn't know what's inside yet. Partly-cloudy dawn: the storm's debris arriving on a clearing shore.

concordance.text: She is alive; behold, / Her eyelids, cases to those heavenly jewels / Which Pericles hath lost, / Begin to part their fringes of bright gold; / The diamonds of a most praised water / Doth appear, to make the world twice rich. Live, / And make us weep to hear your fate, fair creature
concordance.speaker: CERIMON
concordance.context: Cerimon revives Thaisa — her eyes opening like jewels. The storm gave her up for dead; medicine brings her back. Supernatural in the same register as The Winter's Tale: not magic, but something science can't quite explain either.

concordance.text: GOWER: Now sleep yslaked hath the rout; / No din but snores the house about, / Made louder by the o'er-fed breast / Of this most pompous marriage-feast.
concordance.speaker: GOWER
concordance.context: Gower narrating the calm between storms — the wedding feast over, everyone asleep. The stillness before the voyage that will destroy everything. Calm as the weather of ignorance.

concordance.text: GOWER: In Antiochus and his daughter you have heard / Of monstrous lust the due and just reward: / In Pericles, his queen and daughter, seen, / Although assail'd with fortune fierce and keen, / Virtue preserved from fell destruction's blast
concordance.speaker: GOWER
concordance.context: Gower's summary — 'destruction's blast' and 'fortune fierce and keen' as weather of a whole life. The reconciliation is the chorus perspective: looking back, the storms had a shape.

concordance.text: Ay me! poor maid, / Born in a tempest, when my mother died, / This world to me is like a lasting storm, / Whirring me from my friends.
concordance.speaker: MARINA
concordance.context: Marina naming herself — born in a tempest, living in a lasting storm. The weather is biographical: she IS the storm's child. The metaphor isn't decorative; it's identity.

concordance.text: When I was born, the wind was north... / My father, as nurse says, did never fear, / But cried 'Good seamen!' to the sailors, / Galling his kingly hands haling ropes
concordance.speaker: MARINA
concordance.context: Marina retelling her birth — the north wind, her father hauling ropes with his royal hands. The tenderness is toward a father she barely knows, imagined through his hands on the ropes.

concordance.text: HELICANUS: Sir, here is the man / That can, in aught you would, resolve you. / PERICLES: Hail, reverend sir! the gods preserve you! / Hail, royal sir!
concordance.speaker: PERICLES / HELICANUS
concordance.context: Pericles on his ship, still mute with grief — the sea is calm, the evening still. He hasn't spoken in months. The calm is both the weather and his withdrawal from the world.

concordance.text: I am great with woe, and shall deliver weeping. / My dearest wife was like this maid, and such a one / My daughter might have been
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles speaking for the first time — 'great with woe' as pregnancy, delivering weeping. He doesn't know yet that Marina is his daughter. The evening sea is calm; his grief breaks the surface.

concordance.text: O Helicanus, strike me, honoured sir; / Give me a gash, put me to present pain; / Lest this great sea of joys rushing upon me / O'erbear the shores of my mortality, / And drown me with their sweetness.
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles recognizing Marina — joy as a sea that might drown him. After years of real storms, the overwhelming force is happiness. He needs pain to anchor him. Reconciliation as flood.

concordance.text: Give me my robes. I am wild in my beholding. / O heavens bless my girl! But, hark, what music? / Tell Helicanus, my Marina, tell him / O'er, point by point, for yet he seems to doubt, / How sure you are my daughter.
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles hears the music of the spheres — only he can hear it. The evening sea, the reunion, the impossible music: the play's answer to all its storms. Supernatural as grace, not threat.

concordance.text: No more, you gods! your present kindness / Makes my past miseries sports: you shall do well, / That on the touching of her lips I may / Melt and no more be seen.
concordance.speaker: PERICLES
concordance.context: Pericles asking to melt — dissolve into the kiss. Past miseries become 'sports.' The melting is both ice thawing and self dissolving into joy. Clear day, no weather left to fight.

concordance.text: I am thy father's spirit, / Doom'd for a certain term to walk the night, / And for the day confined to fast in fires
concordance.speaker: GHOST
concordance.context: The Ghost's self-description — walking at night, burning by day. Two weathers for two states: cold dark freedom, hot bright punishment. The battlements are the ghost's permitted atmosphere.

concordance.text: The glow-worm shows the matin to be near, / And 'gins to pale his uneffectual fire: / Adieu, adieu! Hamlet, remember me.
concordance.speaker: GHOST
concordance.context: The ghost fading at dawn — glow-worm paling as morning approaches. The supernatural must leave when the weather changes. Pre-dawn is a threshold: the ghost's last moment.

concordance.text: O, that this too too solid flesh would melt / Thaw and resolve itself into a dew!
concordance.speaker: HAMLET
concordance.context: Hamlet's first soliloquy — wanting to melt like frost into dew. The thaw metaphor is weather applied to the body: solid to liquid, form to formlessness. Despair as a wish to become weather rather than person.

concordance.text: How weary, stale, flat and unprofitable, / Seem to me all the uses of this world! / Fie on't! ah fie! 'tis an unweeded garden, / That grows to seed; things rank and gross in nature / Possess it merely.
concordance.speaker: HAMLET
concordance.context: The world as unweeded garden — rank, gross, gone to seed. The overcast isn't weather but it is atmosphere: everything flat, stale, unprofitable. A mind in which nothing has light.

concordance.text: I have of late — but wherefore I know not — lost all my mirth, forgone all custom of exercises; and indeed it goes so heavily with my disposition that this goodly frame, the earth, seems to me a sterile promontory, this most excellent canopy, the air, look you, this brave o'erhanging firmament, this majestical roof fretted with golden fire, why, it appears no other thing to me than a foul and pestilent congregation of vapours.
concordance.speaker: HAMLET
concordance.context: Hamlet's 'congregation of vapours' speech — the sky itself redescribed as foul mist. The firmament is golden fire to others; to him it's pestilent fog. Depression as weather: the same sky, different atmosphere.

concordance.text: There is a willow grows aslant a brook, / That shows his hoar leaves in the glassy stream; / There with fantastic garlands did she come / Of crow-flowers, nettles, daisies, and long purples
concordance.speaker: GERTRUDE
concordance.context: Gertrude narrating Ophelia's drowning — the willow, the glassy stream, the garlands. The water is still, not flowing; the day is light enough to see hoar leaves reflected. Overcast because the light is silver, not gold: willow-light.

concordance.text: There, on the pendent boughs her coronet weeds / Clambering to hang, an envious sliver broke; / When down her weedy trophies and herself / Fell in the weeping brook.
concordance.speaker: GERTRUDE
concordance.context: The branch breaks — Ophelia falls into the 'weeping brook.' The brook weeps; she doesn't. The water is personified but the weather is still: no wind, no storm, just gravity and a rotten branch.

concordance.text: Her clothes spread wide; / And, mermaid-like, awhile they bore her up: / Which time she chanted snatches of old tunes; / As one incapable of her own distress, / Or like a creature native and indued / Unto that element
concordance.speaker: GERTRUDE
concordance.context: Ophelia floating and singing — 'native and indued unto that element.' She becomes water. The mermaid comparison and the singing make it supernatural: not drowning but transformation. The brook is still; she floats.

concordance.text: But long it could not be / Till that her garments, heavy with their drink, / Pull'd the poor wretch from her melodious lay / To muddy death.
concordance.speaker: GERTRUDE
concordance.context: The garments drag her down — 'muddy death.' From mermaid to mud. The weather hasn't changed; the water's nature has. The overcast stillness is what makes the death so quiet.

concordance.text: Imperious Caesar, dead and turn'd to clay, / Might stop a hole to keep the wind away: / O, that that earth, which kept the world in awe, / Should patch a wall to expel the winter's flaw!
concordance.speaker: HAMLET
concordance.context: Caesar's clay patching a wall against the wind — the body becomes weather-proofing. 'Winter's flaw' is a literal blast of cold air. Despair that's also comedy: the greatest man becomes insulation.

concordance.text: Now cracks a noble heart. Good night, sweet prince, / And flights of angels sing thee to thy rest!
concordance.speaker: HORATIO
concordance.context: Horatio's farewell — 'good night' to a dead prince. The play that started in cold midnight ends with a wished-for flight to rest. The clear night is Horatio's hope for Hamlet: angels, not ghosts, this time.

concordance.text: Under the greenwood tree / Who loves to lie with me, / And turn his merry note / Unto the sweet bird's throat, / Come hither, come hither, come hither: / Here shall he see / No enemy / But winter and rough weather.
concordance.speaker: AMIENS
concordance.context: Amiens' greenwood song — the only enemy is winter and rough weather. The song's weather is real (outdoor, exposed) but the mood is cheerful: weather is the only enemy, and it's preferable to human enemies.

concordance.text: Who doth ambition shun / And loves to live i' the sun, / Seeking the food he eats / And pleased with what he gets, / Come hither, come hither, come hither: / Here shall he see / No enemy / But winter and rough weather.
concordance.speaker: AMIENS
concordance.context: Second verse — living in the sun, eating what you find. The weather has shifted to fair within the same song: sun, contentment, simplicity. The 'rough weather' refrain is winter remembered, not winter present.

concordance.text: Truly, I would the gods had made thee poetical... / TOUCHSTONE: I do not know what 'poetical' is: is it honest in deed and word? Is it a true thing?
concordance.speaker: TOUCHSTONE / AUDREY
concordance.context: Touchstone and Audrey in the forest — poetry vs. honesty. A clear day in Arden: the comic subplot has its own weather, which is fair and simple. Touchstone's wit needs no atmosphere.

concordance.text: Hang there, my verse, in witness of my love; / And thou, thrice-crowned queen of night, survey / With thy chaste eye, from thy pale sphere above, / Thy huntress' name that my full life doth sway.
concordance.speaker: ORLANDO
concordance.context: Orlando hanging poems on trees by moonlight — addressing Diana/the moon as witness to his love for Rosalind. The clear night is the poem's native atmosphere: moonlight, forest, lovesickness made public on bark.

concordance.text: From the east to western Ind, / No jewel is like Rosalind. / Her worth, being mounted on the wind, / Through all the world bears Rosalind.
concordance.speaker: ORLANDO [poem]
concordance.context: Orlando's poem found on a tree — Rosalind's worth carried on the wind worldwide. The wind is the poem's courier. Bad verse, genuine feeling, and the forest as bulletin board.

concordance.text: Who ever loved that loved not at first sight?
concordance.speaker: PHEBE [quoting Marlowe]
concordance.context: Phebe quoting Marlowe — love at first sight in a sunlit forest clearing. The pastoral is in full bloom: shepherdesses falling in love, clear weather, open air. The tenderness is literary as much as emotional.
